title: NoSQL Document Store (Generic)
description: Document-oriented NoSQL database that stores JSON-like objects with flexible, schema-optional structure. Well-suited to content management, product catalogs, user profiles, and semi-structured data where the shape varies per record. Common implementations include MongoDB, DynamoDB, and CouchDB.

provides.reasoning: Core capability of document-oriented NoSQL databases. Accurately describes the pattern's primary function of storing and retrieving JSON-like documents.

provides.description: Schema-less data model for rapid iteration
provides.reasoning: Key characteristic of document stores that distinguishes them from relational databases. Documents can have varying structures without requiring predefined schemas.
This pattern is a type of NoSQL database and components may require any NoSQL database capability. Provides important categorization for dependency resolution.
Document stores specifically handle JSON or JSON-like documents (BSON, etc.). This is a more specific capability that some consumers might explicitly need.

requires.reasoning: Database instances require compute resources to run the database engine, process queries, and manage data.
Document stores need persistent storage to durably store documents. This is distinct from the document-storage capability they provide at the application level.
Database services need network connectivity for client connections and potentially for replication in distributed deployments.

consistency_level.description: Read consistency model: strong=immediate consistency with higher latency, eventual=best performance with temporary inconsistency, causal=maintains order of related operations

index_strategy.description: Index creation approach: selective=index based on query patterns (balanced), comprehensive=index all fields (fast reads, slow writes, high storage), minimal=primary key only (fast writes, slow queries)

document_size_limit_mb.description: Maximum size per document in MB. Lower limits encourage better data modeling, higher limits support complex nested structures

sharding_strategy.description: Data distribution strategy: none=single node, hash=even distribution, range=ordered data queries, geo=location-based routing

replication_factor.description: Number of data replicas for high availability and read scaling. Higher values increase durability and read throughput but cost more

backup_retention_days.description: Number of days to retain automated backups for point-in-time recovery

ssl_enabled.description: Enable TLS/SSL for database connections. Should be true in all non-local environments.

provenance.estimatedMonthlyRangeUsd: Generic NoSQL document store pattern with cloud-agnostic implementation. Min $0 assumes free tier usage (MongoDB Atlas free tier 512MB, AWS DynamoDB free tier 25GB/25 WCU/25 RCU, or self-hosted on existing infrastructure). Max $500 represents small-to-medium production workload: ~100GB storage, moderate throughput (1000-5000 requests/min), single-region deployment. Examples: MongoDB Atlas M10 instance ($57/mo) + storage/IOPS, DynamoDB with 100 WCU/RCU + 50GB storage (~$150-200/mo), or managed DocumentDB t3.medium (~$150/mo). Does not include large-scale production which could exceed $5000/mo for high-traffic applications.
provenance.adoptionCost: Medium-high complexity adoption cost. Factors include: (1) Learning document modeling paradigms vs relational databases (denormalization, embedding vs referencing) - 20-30 hours; (2) Understanding consistency models and trade-offs specific to document stores; (3) Implementing proper indexing strategies for query performance; (4) Setting up connection pooling, retry logic, and error handling; (5) Data migration strategy if converting from existing system; (6) Team training on query languages (MongoDB Query Language, DynamoDB expressions, etc.). With AI coding assistants, reduces from traditional $3000-4000 to ~$1500. Assumes 40-60 development hours at blended rate with AI assistance for boilerplate, schema design suggestions, and query optimization.
provenance.licenseCost: Generic pattern with primarily open-source implementations. MongoDB Community Edition is free (SSPL license), CouchDB is Apache 2.0, AWS DynamoDB and Azure Cosmos DB are pay-as-you-go with no license fees. Commercial options exist (MongoDB Enterprise at $5000-10000+/year for advanced features like ops manager, LDAP integration) but not required for base pattern implementation. Self-hosted open-source implementations have zero licensing cost, making this accessible for any budget.

supports_nfr.reason: Document stores like DynamoDB provides up to 99.999% availability across multiple regions.
Document stores have inherent latency floor of 10ms at p95 due to document parsing, indexing, and network overhead.
Document stores require at least 50ms at p99 for complex queries with secondary indexes and document scanning.
Document stores like DynamoDB enables rapid recovery with automatic failover achieving RTO of at least 1 minute..
Document stores can support up to 100K read queries per second with proper indexing and connection pooling.
Document stores can support up to 100K write queries per second before requiring sharding or specialized variants.
Document stores support multiple tenant isolation strategies including document-level, collection-per-tenant, and database-per-tenant.
Document stores can handle PII through field-level encryption, tokenization, data masking, and access controls.
Document stores support GDPR compliance through field-level encryption, data masking, deletion APIs, and audit trails.
Document stores can be configured for HIPAA compliance with encryption at rest/in-transit, audit logging, and access controls.
CCPA compliance achievable through data inventory capabilities, deletion APIs for right-to-be-forgotten, and audit trails.
SOX compliance supported through audit trails, immutable logging, access controls, and data retention policies.
Read-your-writes consistency is achievable by reading from primary or using causal consistency mechanisms in document stores.
Document stores support strict durability through write concerns requiring acknowledgment from majority of replicas.

supports_constraints.reason: Pattern explicitly provides document-oriented NoSQL database capability as its primary purpose.

requires_constraints.reason: Generic document store pattern is cloud-agnostic and serves as base for specific cloud-managed implementations.
Document databases are infrastructure components with client drivers available for all major programming languages.
Document databases are backend infrastructure accessible from any application platform via network protocols.
Document stores can serve as primary data layer with or without complementary caching infrastructure.
Document stores can be used in both synchronous request-reply and asynchronous event-driven architectures.
Document stores can store AI model metadata, inference results, and training data alongside application data.
Document stores support both single-tenant and multi-tenant architectures through flexible schema and isolation strategies.
Document stores support both real-time transactional access and batch processing through aggregation pipelines.
Document stores support document-level ACID transactions with some implementations offering multi-document transactions.
Document stores can participate in real-time streaming architectures through change streams and event triggers.
Some document stores support vector search capabilities for embedding similarity while others require dedicated vector databases.
Document stores can be accessed as key-value stores using document ID lookups while also supporting richer queries.
Document stores can model graph relationships through embedded documents and references but are not optimized for deep graph traversal.
Document stores can store time-series data using appropriate schema design though specialized time-series databases are more efficient.
Document stores excel at OLTP workloads with document-level operations but also support analytical queries.
Document stores support analytical workloads through aggregation frameworks and indexing though dedicated OLAP systems are more efficient.

requires_nfr.reason: Document stores like DynamoDB provides up to 99.999% availability across multiple regions.
Document stores have inherent latency floor of 10ms at p95 due to document parsing, indexing, and network overhead.
Document stores require at least 50ms at p99 for complex queries with secondary indexes and document scanning.
Document stores like DynamoDB enables rapid recovery with automatic failover achieving RTO of at least 1 minute..
Synchronous replication with majority write concern enables zero data loss (RPO=0) for document stores.
Document stores like DynamoDB auto-scaling supports extremely high throughput up to 100M requests/sec (360B jobs/hour).
Document stores can support up to 100K read queries per second with proper indexing and connection pooling.
Document stores can support up to 100K write queries per second before requiring sharding or specialized variants.
